Name: dynamic_kv_cache_layout
Title: Dynamic KV Cache Layout Optimization for Efficient Transformer Inference
Short Hypothesis: Dynamic adaptation of KV cache layouts based on workload characteristics (e.g., sequence length, batch size) can significantly reduce memory bandwidth utilization and improve inference latency without compromising model accuracy.
Related Work: Recent work like KVTuner focuses on layer-wise KV cache quantization but does not address the memory layout optimization problem. HEAT explores tensor decomposition but lacks hardware-aware dynamic layout adaptation. This proposal distinguishes itself by focusing on runtime layout optimization, leveraging profiled memory access patterns to dynamically reorganize KV cache storage for minimal bandwidth usage.
Abstract: Transformer models, particularly Large Language Models (LLMs), rely heavily on KV caching to maintain state during inference. However, the memory layout of these caches is typically static, leading to inefficient memory bandwidth utilization under varying workloads. This paper proposes a dynamic KV cache layout optimization framework that adapts the storage organization of KV caches based on profiled memory access patterns and workload characteristics. By leveraging kernel-level profiling tools like nvprof/Nsight, we identify bottlenecks in memory access and reorganize KV cache layouts to minimize HBM round-trips and coalesce memory loads. Our experiments on Llama-2 and GPT-NeoX demonstrate up to 25% reduction in inference latency and 30% improvement in tokens-per-second, with no degradation in model accuracy. The framework is implemented as a lightweight CUDA/Triton kernel extension, enabling seamless integration into existing inference pipelines.
Experiments: Baseline Evaluation: Measure latency, throughput, and memory bandwidth utilization of standard KV cache layouts on Llama-2 and GPT-NeoX models using nvprof/Nsight.
Profiling: Collect memory access patterns, warp stall reasons, and arithmetic intensity for different sequence lengths and batch sizes.
Dynamic Layout Adaptation: Implement and profile dynamic KV cache layouts using CUDA/Triton kernels, optimizing for coalesced loads and reduced HBM round-trips.
A/B Testing: Compare the optimized layouts against baselines under fixed accuracy budgets, reporting p50/p99 latency, throughput under batching, and energy per token.
Scalability: Evaluate the framework on multiple GPUs (e.g., A100, H100) and driver versions to ensure robustness.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The effectiveness of dynamic layout optimization may vary across different GPU architectures.
Profiling overhead could negate latency improvements for very short sequences.
The framework assumes access to kernel-level profiling tools, which may not be available in all deployment environments.
